video_title: SpaceX: Start of the Next Megabubble or End of the Rally?
gist: On the day SpaceX IPOs at a ~$1.77T valuation (94x revenue), the hosts argue mega-trend and 'Elon multiple' dynamics make shorting it career risk, lay out a low-float airdrop-style trade plan (buy a dip back toward the $135 IPO price for a ~50% bounce), and warn that future OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs will be bearish for indices via capital rotation. They also pitch chips (Intel, SanDisk), uranium, Robinhood over Hype, and collectibles as preferred mega-trend exposures.

insights.claim: SpaceX IPO'd at roughly a $1.77 trillion valuation, with shares moving from $135 to $177 in about 35 seconds and holding the highs of the day, indicating little sell-side supply has come online.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Confirms a successful debut with no early insider dump; watch whether supply stays scarce into the first unlock windows.
insights.quote: Thing goes from 135 to 177 a share in 35 seconds.

insights.claim: SpaceX trades at about 94x revenue, but a sky-high price-to-sales ratio is not a reason to short a company building into a mega-trend; the multiple compresses over time as revenue scales, as happened with Nvidia (P/S ~45 at $47 in 2023).
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Reframes valuation: in mega-trend names, growth outruns the multiple, so high P/S alone is not a short thesis.
insights.quote: You're safe until you prove to the market that you can't deliver on what you promised. But until then, you're safe.

insights.claim: SpaceX is effectively a low-float, high-FDV stock priced 'on vibes' at ~$2T simply because Elon Musk said it was worth that, demonstrating his unmatched ability to achieve the lowest cost of capital of any entrepreneur.
insights.speaker: Jonah (co-host)
insights.so_what: The 'Elon multiple' and low float mean valuation is narrative-driven, not fundamentals-driven; size positions accordingly.
insights.quote: Elon is the best in the world at achieving the lowest cost of capital like this thing is no other entrepreneur could pull this off.

insights.claim: Major index funds (MSCI, NASDAQ, Russell) are forced buyers of SpaceX over the first ~15 days, making it a low-float high-FDV asset with structural inflows rather than outflows, so it should not be shorted.
insights.speaker: Jonah (co-host)
insights.so_what: Forced index buying creates a mechanical bid; shorting against passive inflows is unfavorable near term.
insights.quote: shorting Elon is genuinely career risk

insights.claim: Retail allocation in the SpaceX IPO was cut from a planned 30% of the float to about 20%, leaving many retail bids unfilled (Robinhood filled best) and creating pent-up retail demand to chase any dip.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Unfilled retail demand is latent buying power that can fuel a bounce off lower levels.
insights.quote: they originally were going to have 30% of the float allocated to retail and I think they got down to 20

insights.claim: Banks underwriting the SpaceX IPO collected roughly $500 million in aggregate fees, and bullish sell-side projections (e.g., Goldman Sachs invoking Mars cities) are conflicted because the banks want the business.
insights.speaker: Jonah (co-host)
insights.so_what: Treat euphoric underwriter price targets as marketing, not independent analysis.
insights.quote: I think all the banks in aggregate pulled out 500 million

insights.claim: SpaceX may trade like an airdrop: dump the small online supply first, then a mega-rally, then heavy insider/employee unlocks in roughly 3-6 months before settling at a reasonable price; the base case is a ~25% drop before any further upside.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Maps a path for entry timing; the big supply (insiders) hits months out, so patience beats chasing.
insights.quote: This could look very much like an airdrop trade actually where you basically just chuck out of the supply that's online right now.

insights.claim: A SpaceX pullback to the $135 IPO price would be a 'screaming buy' worth an 8-10% position for a quick ~50% bounce, with scaling in starting around $140-142 and reselling near $160-170.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Concrete actionable levels: accumulate near the $135 IPO anchor, target a 50% swing trade.
insights.quote: If we get to 135, that is a screaming buy.

insights.claim: The preferred post-IPO playbook is to sit on your hands and wait until insiders have far too much to sell (to fund SF/NY/LA real estate) and then buy the dip, mirroring the post-Facebook-IPO collapse buying opportunity.
insights.speaker: Jonah (co-host)
insights.so_what: Insider selling creates the best entry; the playbook is to buy after the unlock-driven washout, not at debut.
insights.quote: wait till the insiders have way too much to sell so they can buy their SF New York and LA real estate and then um then buy

insights.claim: An SPV offering day-one SpaceX liquidity (from XAI shares that converted via SpaceX's acquisition of XAI) charged a 30% fee on the first day's price appreciation and lacked any share-delivery guarantee, creating risk of owing a huge fee while the stock falls before shares settle.
insights.speaker: Jonah (co-host)
insights.so_what: Illustrates predatory private-market access structures; scrutinize SPV fee terms and delivery mechanics before chasing pre-IPO exposure.
insights.quote: 30% of first day price appreciation is I've never heard such a horrific SPV

insights.claim: Hyperliquid arbitrageurs successfully aligned the SpaceX pre-IPO price with the actual IPO price, positioning Hyperliquid as a venue that democratizes access to previously accredited-only private assets.
insights.speaker: Jonah (co-host)
insights.so_what: Hyperliquid's pre-IPO price discovery is a structural use case; watch it as the liquidity venue for future private-to-public listings.
insights.quote: Hyperlid's going to democratize access to a lot of what was previously inaccessible stuff.

insights.claim: SpaceX is positioned to be the only company able to mine asteroids within ~20 years and could become the largest company in the world by monopolizing space-extracted materials, on top of Starlink and defense optionality.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Frames the long-term bull case as multi-vertical (resources, telecom, defense), justifying a structural hold despite a possible 50% drawdown.
insights.quote: SpaceX itself is probably going to be the only company in the world that can mine asteroids in the next 20 years.

insights.claim: Starlink is a credible direct competitor to Verizon, AT&T and Comcast because the product is better, and SpaceX also provides strategic military advantages as demonstrated in the Ukraine war.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Telecom disruption and defense relevance are underappreciated revenue legs supporting the valuation; watch Starlink subscriber and contract growth.
insights.quote: Starlink could quite literally take on Verizon, AT&T, Comcast because it is better.

insights.claim: Markets are structurally more optimistic than pessimistic, so traders should never short assets with potential to 'conquer the world' because bears consistently lose against belief in the American dream until a company proves it cannot deliver.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: A bias rule: avoid shorting dominant mega-trend narratives; wait for proof of failure, not just stretched valuation.
insights.quote: Bears die. Bears consistently die because people believe in the American dream.

insights.claim: Upcoming OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs will likely be bearish events for the broad market and indices because new supply in the AI vertical drains liquidity from existing AI-adjacent names (Google, Amazon, the Mag 7).
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Position for index weakness around AI IPO events; capital rotation, not fundamentals, drives the drawdown.
insights.quote: the OpenAI and Anthropic IPO will be a bearish event for the market

insights.claim: Because OpenAI and Anthropic are unprofitable they likely won't be added to the NASDAQ index, so capital flowing into them and out of index constituents can create net wealth while indices mechanically decline.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Index-level performance can diverge from total market wealth; don't read falling indices as falling AI value.
insights.quote: the indexes by virtue of construction are not going to do well in my personal opinion

insights.claim: On SpaceX IPO day, other space stocks (Rocket Lab/RKLB, Virgin Galactic/SPCE) fell ~30% as the 'liquidity suck' reversed a retail-driven run-up that had doubled Virgin Galactic into the event, showing piggyback narrative trades unwind on the main listing.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Pre-IPO narrative pumps in adjacent tickers are exit liquidity; fade or avoid them and don't stay short after the multi-sigma flush.
insights.quote: every other space company is down 30% today

insights.claim: Google struck a deal to buy 3 million TPUs from Intel by 2028, diversifying away from sole reliance on TSMC and signaling Intel is finally making competitive chips after decades of lagging.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: A major anchor foundry customer re-rates Intel's competitive story; treat it as a catalyst for the Intel long.
insights.quote: they struck a deal with Intel to go buy 3 million units from Intel by 2028

insights.claim: The chip trade is a better risk/reward than SpaceX because Intel (around $600B) has more room to reach ~$1 trillion than SpaceX has to go from $1.7T to $3T, and SanDisk has rebounded from ~$400 toward all-time highs near $2,000.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Prefer Intel and SanDisk over SpaceX for upside-to-cap; the doubling path is mathematically easier off a lower base.
insights.quote: I think it's very difficult to go from 1.7 trillion to 3 trillion whereas I think Intel could still get to one

insights.claim: The speaker is redeploying cash into uranium after a recent sell-off, citing an apparent de-escalation with Iran and a market that keeps printing as reasons to flip back to short-term bullishness.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Uranium dips are being bought on easing geopolitical risk; a watch item for energy/commodity exposure.
insights.quote: I'm also rebying uranium rebying a lot of uranium after that after that sell-off

insights.claim: The first Fed meeting under Kevin Warsh is a key near-term event; nothing is expected to change (~99% chance), but his tone, forward guidance, and willingness to consider a hike cycle could reset the market regime.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Watch Warsh's first presser for hawkish hike-cycle signals that could undo the current melt-up.
insights.quote: we get the first Fed meeting with Kevin Worsh

themes.theme: Crypto views: L1s, monero, Hood vs Hype

insights.claim: Crypto L1 infrastructure tokens are largely 'cabals' and grifts driven by insiders (with Aptos called a complete grift and skepticism extended to Canton), while Solana is credited as genuinely building real financial products.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Differentiate within L1s: avoid insider-heavy infra tokens, favor those shipping real financial products like Solana.
insights.quote: the things like Aptos were just I think basically a complete grift from from the start

insights.claim: After a Zcash bug raised fears of infinite ZEC printing, ZEC spiked then sold off ~30%; any 5-6 standard-deviation news-driven move should trigger closing out winning trades because the increasingly efficient market tends to revert such moves.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: A risk rule: exit profitable positions on extreme multi-sigma news spikes rather than pressing them.
insights.quote: anytime you get a massive five standard deviation move like that, close out every trade that you have on that asset

insights.claim: Robinhood (HOOD) is favored over Hyperliquid (HYPE) for the next 6-12 months because HOOD has more diversified income streams and lock-ins like Trump accounts; HOOD is held as a potential 10-bagger up to $1,000, while HYPE is no longer seen as a 10-bagger.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Pair-trade signal: prefer HOOD over HYPE; the upgrade trigger for HYPE would be a CFTC deal and a competitive consumer fintech app.
insights.quote: I think Hood could be a 10bagger still. So I'm I'm in I'm in a hood until until a thousand bucks.

insights.claim: Ethena (ENA) at all-time lows is not a viable long-term asset; the speaker bets it does not exist in five years because a yield-bearing stablecoin model is a dated 2023 idea.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Avoid ENA as a structural hold despite the low price; any move is likely a trade, not an investment.
insights.quote: My bets in five years it doesn't exist.

insights.claim: Collectibles (Pokemon, Yu-Gi-Oh, sports/TCG cards, Roman coins, cars) are a mega-trend driven by a boomer-to-Gen-Z/millennial wealth transfer, with some cards, coins, and cars expected to outperform the stock market over the next two years.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Demographic wealth transfer is a multi-year tailwind for collectibles; an edge-based allocation away from index funds.
insights.quote: I think that collectibles are a mega trend.

insights.claim: Index funds will underperform active selection over the coming decade, making this 'a decade of hedge funds, VC funds and private markets,' with the average successful person now doing ~15-20x more angel investing volume than ten years ago.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Argues alpha is migrating to private markets and active strategies; reduce passive index reliance if you have edge.
insights.quote: I do genuinely believe that index funds will underperform actual active selection.

insights.claim: Quant funds are likely to keep outperforming because the market is more retail-driven than ever, so gaining exposure to quant strategies is recommended.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: Heavy retail participation is alpha for systematic strategies; consider quant fund allocations.
insights.quote: quant funds are probably going to continue to crush it

insights.claim: The recommended core strategy is to trim around the edges when assets look overheated and rebuild major positions in mega-trend assets held for 3-5 years; for most retail, simply holding uranium, Intel, SanDisk and Google and trading SpaceX at target beats active trading.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: A concrete allocation blueprint: mega-trend core, trim overheated edges, minimal active churn.
insights.quote: it is much better to trim around the edges when things look scary or overheated and just try to rebuild major positions in mega trend assets

insights.claim: Effective trading requires treating P&L as irrelevant ('a disease of the mind'), re-evaluating every position daily with fresh eyes, and building the resilience to sell and rebuy higher rather than anchoring on lost trades.
insights.speaker: Obby (host)
insights.so_what: A discipline framework: focus on forward price, not realized P&L, to avoid anchoring-driven mistakes.
insights.quote: P&L is a disease of the mind.

insights.claim: The social contract is being broken because so much wealth is being captured in private markets, where capital holders hoard returns generated by high-productivity workers while non-accredited investors (without ~$1M) are locked out.
insights.speaker: host(s)
insights.so_what: Signals rising political/regulatory pressure to democratize private-market access (e.g., via vehicles like Hyperliquid).
insights.quote: they're hoarding all of the wealth generated by the high functioning, high production people in our society

insights.claim: Cash is 'trash' because dollars keep being printed, pushing retail to chase narrative assets like space stocks, which explains the indiscriminate buying and the FOMO driving the current melt-up.
insights.speaker: Jonah (co-host)
insights.so_what: Persistent debasement fear is the structural driver of risk-asset inflows; expect continued narrative chasing until liquidity tightens.
insights.quote: cash is trash. You can't just save your money anymore because they keep printing uh dollars.

insights.claim: The simultaneous arrival of SpaceX insider unlocks and the OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs would concentrate a large wave of selling, as 20-year SpaceX holders seek liquidity at the same time new AI supply hits the market.
insights.speaker: Jonah (co-host)
insights.so_what: Flags a potential clustered supply shock; calendar-watch the overlap of SpaceX unlocks and AI IPOs for market-wide pressure.
insights.quote: all those big boys unlock and try to get their liquidity they've been waiting 20 years for right at the same time as Open AI and Anthropic come to market
